1: The structural transition at 40:1 marks the end of YHWH’s first speech. The formula vaya’an YHWH et-’iyyov (YHWH answered/responded to Job) opens both speeches (38:1 and Job 40:6); its recurrence here marks a clear formal division in the divine speeches into two rounds. The first speech (Job 38:1–Job 40:5) covered cosmology, meteorology, and zoology; the second speech (Job 40:6–Job 41:34) will focus on the two great chaos-creatures: Behemoth and Leviathan. The legal vocabulary (’anah, answer/respond) frames the entire divine discourse as YHWH’s formal legal reply to Job’s petition.
2: The challenge — “Shall a fault-finder contend with (yariv) Shaddai?” — uses the legal-dispute vocabulary that has pervaded the book. The riv (legal complaint/lawsuit) is precisely what Job has threatened to bring against God (Job 13:3: “I desire to argue my case with God”; Job 23:4: “I would lay my case before him”). YHWH now formally invites the riv: let the one who argues with God answer! The mokhiah (reprover/fault-finder) who contends with God is the one who takes the role of legal challenger; YHWH accepts the challenge and immediately turns it back.
3: Job’s response to YHWH (vv3–5) is the book’s first verbal response from Job to the divine speeches. The brevity is striking: the man who filled 29+ chapters with sustained argument is reduced to two verses. The structural contrast between Job’s speeches (multi-chapter, extensively argued) and his response to YHWH (four short phrases) is a formal demonstration of what the divine speeches have accomplished: not by refuting Job’s arguments but by revealing a reality so vastly larger than the argumentative framework that the arguments become irrelevant.
4: “Behold, I am of small account (qallotiy); what shall I answer you? I lay my hand on my mouth” — the hand-over-mouth gesture is the ANE posture of submission before divine authority. In Mesopotamian royal iconography, the devoted suppliant before the king raised a hand before the mouth in the gesture of reverent silence. In Ugaritic texts, human figures before the gods are depicted in similar postures. Isaiah 6:5: Isaiah’s “woe is me, I am undone” at the throne-room vision; the prophet falls silent before divine holiness. Job’s gesture enacts the wisdom tradition’s principle: there is a time for every word and a time for silence (Proverbs 17:28: even a fool who keeps silent is considered wise).
5: “I have spoken once, and I will not answer; twice, but I will proceed no further” — the “once” and “twice” formula is a common ANE literary pattern for climactic emphasis: Ugaritic texts use the “once…twice” formula (Akkadian: ishten…shinaateni) in wisdom sayings; the biblical x…x+1 formula appears in Proverbs 30:15–31 (the graded numerical sayings) and throughout Amos (Job 1:3,6,9,11: “for three transgressions and for four”). Job’s “once/twice” is not an exact count but the rhetorical formula for “completely, definitively.” He has said all he will say; the argument is closed on his side.
6: YHWH’s second speech begins with the identical introduction as the first (Job 38:1): “Then the LORD spoke to Job out of the whirlwind.” The se’arah (whirlwind/storm) is the recognized form of theophanic appearance in the OT. Elijah was taken up in a whirlwind (2 Kings 2:1,11); Ezekiel’s inaugural vision came “out of the north, a great cloud with brightness around it and fire flashing forth continually” (Ezekiel 1:4). In Mesopotamian theology, the storm-god Enlil communicated through storms; the Akkadian noun assatu (storm-whirlwind) was associated with divine anger-manifestation. The deliberate repetition of 38:1 signals that Job’s submission (vv3–5) has not ended the divine speech; YHWH has more to say.
7: The renewed command — “Dress for action like a man; I will question you and you make it known to me” — repeats 38:3 verbatim. The phrase ’ezer na keggever (gird your loins/dress for action like a gever) uses the gever (strong man/warrior) form of the word for man, not the ordinary ’ish. The military gesture of girding loins prepared a man for battle or strenuous action by pulling the long outer garment up and tucking it into a belt. Elijah girded his loins before running ahead of Ahab’s chariot (1 Kings 18:46); the Passover was eaten with loins girded (Exodus 12:11). YHWH repeats his challenge: stand up and face what is about to come.
8: The second speech’s challenge takes a different form from the cosmological questions: “Will you even put me in the wrong? Will you condemn me that you may be in the right?” This is the legal crux. Job’s sustained argument for his own innocence has implied that if Job is innocent and God is allowing/causing his suffering, then God is in the wrong. YHWH names this implication directly: are you condemning me (tarshe’eni) in order to justify yourself (lema’an titsdak)? The judicial vocabulary is explicit: rasha’ (condemn as guilty) and tsadaq (vindicate/justify). The divine legal challenge is not “stop asking questions” but “face the implications of what your legal claims require.”
9: “Have you an arm like God, and can you thunder with a voice like his?” — the divine attributes of arm (zero’a) and thunder (ra’am) are the two primary expressions of divine power in ANE theology. The divine arm is the instrument of creation and military victory: Exodus 15:16 (the arm of YHWH that redeems Israel); Isaiah 51:9 (the arm that cut Rahab in pieces). The divine thunder-voice is the storm-theophany: Ps 29 is a sustained meditation on YHWH’s thunder as the voice that breaks cedars, shakes the wilderness, and strips forests. In Ugaritic theology, Baal’s thunder was his voice; YHWH appropriates this attribute as his own.
10: The challenge to Job to “deck yourself with majesty (ga’on) and dignity (gova); clothe yourself with glory (hod) and splendor (hadar)” uses the vocabulary of royal investiture. In the ancient world, kings were visibly distinguished from ordinary people by their garments, crowns, and insignia; the divine king was similarly clothed in cosmic garments. Psalms 93:1: “The LORD reigns; he is robed in majesty; the LORD is robed; he has put on strength as his belt.” Psalms 104:1–2: “You are clothed with splendor and majesty, covering yourself with light as with a garment.” YHWH challenges Job to don the divine royal costume — a challenge only one person in the book is in fact qualified to meet.
11: The challenge to pour out the overflowings of anger (shophekhey evratekha) and look on every proud one and abase him reflects the divine role as the one who humbles the proud. The theme of YHWH humbling the proud and lifting up the lowly (1 Samuel 2:7–8; Psalms 75:7; Proverbs 3:34; Isaiah 2:12–17) is one of the most consistent themes in OT theology. The proud ones (ge’im) are those who assert themselves above their creaturely status; the divine response to human pride is humiliation. The challenge to Job: if you can do what God does — humble every proud one — then you are qualified to critique divine justice.
12: Treading down the wicked where they stand and hiding them in the dust together (tehbem be’afar yakhad), binding their faces in the hidden place, is the complete execution of retributive justice against the proud and wicked. YHWH’s rhetorical challenge is: if Job can execute justice by himself against all the wicked simultaneously, then Job’s complaint that God has failed to do so would be self-refuting — Job himself would have proven that the task is within a being’s power. The argument reveals the scale problem in Job’s complaint: the cosmic administration of justice is not a task that admits of the simple standard Job has applied to it.
13: The description of hiding the proud in the dust and binding their faces in the hidden place uses the imagery of complete defeat and burial. In ANE warfare, the defeated were sometimes buried face-down as a mark of disgrace; the binding of the face was the posture of total submission. The “hidden place” (batamuhn, the place of concealment) may refer to the underworld/Sheol where the dead are hidden from the living. The entire challenge (vv10–14) amounts to: become God, execute universal perfect justice against all evil simultaneously, and then your criticism of God’s administration of justice will be self-consistent.
14: The conditional promise — “then will I also praise you to you that your own right hand can save you” (todeh lekha yeminekha toshia’ lakh) — is the culminating irony of the second speech’s opening challenge. If Job can execute perfect retributive justice against all the proud and wicked, then Job’s own hand can save him. In the OT, the divine right hand is the instrument of salvation (Exodus 15:6: “your right hand, O LORD, glorious in power, your right hand, O LORD, shatters the enemy”; Psalms 20:6). The challenge implies its own answer: Job’s right hand cannot execute justice against all evil; therefore it cannot save him; therefore he needs the divine right hand that can.
15: The transition to Behemoth (behemat) is introduced with the remarkable phrase asher asiti immakh — “which I made with you.” Behemoth was made with Job; both are creatures of the same Creator. This co-creaturely relationship is the frame for the description: YHWH is not simply describing a terrifying animal to humble Job but pointing to a co-creature whose existence Job has not considered. Behemoth is almost certainly the hippopotamus (Hippopotamus amphibius), the largest land animal in the ancient world familiar to Israelites through Egypt. Hippos lived in the Nile and in the lower Jordan River until the medieval period.
16: Behemoth’s strength in his loins (koho bemotnav) and power in the muscles of his belly (ve’onov bishrirey vitno) reflects the hippopotamus’s actual physical characteristics: the hippo is the third-largest land animal on earth (after elephant and rhinoceros), weighing up to 3,000 kg, with enormous muscular power concentrated in its torso. In Egyptian religion, the hippopotamus had a complex mythological role: the male hippo was associated with Set, the god of chaos and disorder (a dangerous animal); the female hippo was represented as Taweret, the benevolent goddess of fertility and childbirth. The hippo was simultaneously a chaos-figure and a protective-deity figure.
17: Behemoth stiffening his tail like a cedar (“he stiffens his tail like a cedar; the sinews of his thighs are knit together”) is an anatomically precise description. The hippopotamus has a small but muscular tail which it wags to spread dung as territorial marking; the description of stiffening suggests the tail in its fully extended territorial display. The cedar comparison uses the tree associated with permanence, strength, and royal grandeur (Solomon’s cedars of Lebanon; Psalms 92:12: the righteous flourish like a cedar). The hippo’s apparently incongruous small tail stiffened to cedar-like rigidity demonstrates the unexpected distribution of power in created creatures.
18: Behemoth’s bones as bronze tubes (atsamav afiqei nehusheh) and his limbs like bars of iron (geramav kemin barzel) uses the metals of warfare as comparisons for biological structural strength. In the ancient world, bronze was the primary metal of weapons and armor (from ca. 3300 BCE); iron superseded it in the Iron Age (ca. 1200 BCE onward). The weapons-metal comparison for animal bone and limb strength frames the hippo in the vocabulary of military power — the thing humanity most prided itself on in the ancient world. The hippo’s bones are weapons-grade; its strength exceeds manufactured military strength.
19: Behemoth as “the first of God’s works” (re’shit darkhey ’el) and “made to rule over his companions” uses creation-priority language. In ANE creation theology, the first-created held primacy; the Akkadian term for the first-created among gods or creatures denoted priority of dignity. YHWH’s description places Behemoth at the head of the animal creation — first, greatest, made to be the chief. The “one who made him can approach him with the sword” (only the creator can threaten the first-creation) establishes the Creator-creature asymmetry as the fundamental relationship that governs Behemoth’s place.
20: The mountains yielding food for Behemoth with all the wild animals playing nearby describes the ecological niche of the hippo in its riverine environment. Hippos graze at night on riverbank vegetation, consuming up to 35kg of grass per night; during daylight they rest in water. The mountains bringing tribute is hyperbolic for the abundant vegetation of the Nile and Jordan floodplains where hippos fed. The “wild animals playing around him” reflects the reality of hippo-dominated aquatic environments where other animals shared the water and banks with hippos.
21: Behemoth lying under lotus plants in the cover of the reeds and in the marsh (tahat tse’elim yishkav beseter qaneh ubitsah) is a precise ecological description of hippopotamus habitat. The lotus (tse’el, the Egyptian lotus or water lily) and the reed/papyrus (qaneh) were the characteristic plants of the Nile delta and its tributaries; papyrus marshes (tsah) were the hippo’s natural concealment environment. In Egyptian art, the hippo is frequently depicted in papyrus swamps; Pharaoh Amenhotep III documented a royal hippo hunt in the papyrus swamps of the Fayum. Job’s audience familiar with Egypt would recognize this habitat.
22: The shade of the lotus plants covering Behemoth and the willows of the brook surrounding him describes the concealment of the hippopotamus in its aquatic environment. The willow (arvey nahal, willows of the stream/brook) grows along watercourses throughout the Levant and Egypt; in Psalms 137:2 the exiles hung their harps on the willows of Babylon. The natural canopy of lotus, reed, and willow over a resting hippopotamus creates an enclosure that gives even this enormous animal apparent invisibility. The theological point: YHWH designed concealment for the creature that needs no concealment — another example of divine provision beyond functional necessity.
23: Behemoth’s confidence even when the Jordan surges into his mouth (hen ya’ashok nahar lo yahfoz, the river rages but he is not frightened; he is secure though Jordan floods into his mouth) places the hippo in its Near Eastern context. The Jordan River’s seasonal flooding (Joshua 3:15: “the Jordan overflows all its banks throughout the time of harvest”) was the annual reality of the floodplain. Hippos were present in the lower Jordan Valley in biblical times; archaeological evidence confirms hippo bones in Jordan River deposits. The hippo’s physiological design — semi-aquatic, with eyes and nostrils on top of the skull for surface breathing while submerged — makes flooding an irrelevance rather than a threat.
24: The rhetorical question — can one take Behemoth when he is on watch, pierce his nose with a snare? — establishes the impossibility of human domination over Behemoth. The hippo hunt was one of pharaoh’s prerogatives in Egypt; special religious and political significance attached to the royal hippo hunt (the ritual defeat of Set/chaos-in-hippo-form by the pharaoh represented cosmic order triumphing over chaos). But ordinary human hunters could not take the hippo. The divine question to Job: can you catch Behemoth? The implied answer is no; only the Creator who made him can approach him (v19). The Creator’s unique access to the creature he made is the theological point.
